summary: DHH expresses disappointment that Apple chose not to launch the iTunes Music Store in Denmark, despite widespread rumors and expectations. Apple's country manager explained that only Euro-zone countries were included in the expansion. DHH questions whether Denmark's rejection of the Euro currency or Apple's unwillingness to create a workaround is more to blame. He frames the situation as a betrayal of loyal Danish Apple fans.
key_points: Apple launched iTunes Music Store in Euro-zone countries but excluded Denmark
Denmark's decision to vote against adopting the Euro had unexpected consequences for digital commerce
Apple made exceptions for non-Euro countries like Great Britain but not Denmark
The buildup of rumors and media coverage made the exclusion feel like a deliberate slight
money_quotes: Apple have chosen to route around Denmark and not launch the iTunes Music Store here.
I don't know who to blame more: The people who voted against the Euro in Denmark or Apple for screwing us out of a special solution, like Great Britain got.
This is a dark, dark day for Danish Apple fans.
To tease, to veil in mystery, and then not to deliver is cruel and unusual punishment to loyal subjects.
key_insight: National policy decisions like currency adoption can have unexpected downstream effects on access to digital services and technology platforms.
